hypothesis:
Iteration 9 proved the dual momentum concept has genuine, non-overfit alpha (OOS > IS Sharpe, 7/7 WF windows, 0.0 delta sensitivity) but catastrophically fails on trade count — only 13.875 IS trades. Root cause: with a 63-day lookback, the top-3 rankings are extremely sticky (the same 3 ETFs dominate for weeks). 

The fix is a two-pronged attack on trade frequency: (1) Use DAILY rebalancing (rebalance_days=1) to check rankings every day — but unlike iter 7 which failed on costs with daily rebalancing, this iteration holds only TOP 1 ETF (not top 3). Holding only 1 position means each rank change = a trade. With 8 ETFs and daily ranking, the #1 ETF changes ~2-3x per week. (2) Shorten the momentum lookback to 10 days — a 10-day return ranking on 8 ETFs changes the top-ranked ETF frequently (every 3-5 days on average), generating 50-200 trades over 5 years. (3) No absolute momentum filter — proven to suppress trade count in iters 6/7/8. (4) Long-only to avoid short-side losses (iter 4 failure). 

Why holding top 1 instead of top 3? With top-3, the rankings rarely change because 3 out of 8 positions can be maintained even with some rank shuffling. With top-1, ANY rank change among the top bucket triggers a trade. A 10-day lookback will generate frequent rank changes. The cost impact: fewer but larger trades (100% reallocation per signal) vs iter 7's small incremental rebalances. The key insight is that each 100% reallocation generates exactly 2 trades (sell old, buy new). With ~25-50 rank changes per year in the top position, this gives 50-200 trades over 5 years — satisfying the 50-trade minimum. The 10-day momentum window captures genuine short-term momentum (confirmed in crypto and equity literature) while being responsive enough to generate frequent rank changes.

evaluation.post_mortem: Failed walk-forward gate: only 0 of 7 windows passed (consistency score 1.00)
evaluation.next_direction: Strategy underperforms out-of-sample across multiple time periods — likely overfit or regime-dependent.
